original_abstract: While neural forecasting has driven significant advancements in large-scale predictive systems, accurate long-horizon forecasting remains a formidable challenge. Most current models struggle with high prediction volatility and prohibitive computational costs as the forecast window extends. To address these limitations, we introduce N-HITS, a neural architecture that utilizes hierarchical interpolation and multi-rate data sampling. By decomposing the input signal into various frequencies and scales, the model assembles its forecast sequentially, prioritizing different temporal components at each stage. We provide theoretical evidence that hierarchical interpolation effectively approximates arbitrarily long horizons, provided the signal maintains a degree of smoothness. Extensive experiments on benchmark long-horizon datasets demonstrate that N-HITS significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art models. Notably, our method achieves an average accuracy improvement of nearly 20% over recent Transformer-based architectures while reducing computational overhead by a factor of 50. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/Nixtla/neuralforecast.
humanized_abstract: Many large-scale forecasting applications have benefited from recent advances in neural forecasting. However, one common, yet still unsolved, issue is the accurate forecasting of very long horizons. Indeed, large forecast volatility and high computational cost for long horizons are major barriers in current forecasting practices. In this paper, we propose a new neural architecture called N-HITS: Neural Hierarchical Interpolation with multi-rates Sampling. Our approach can break down any input signal to different frequency and scale components, which can then be used to sequentially assemble the final forecast by focusing at each time on the most relevant horizons in each component. We theoretically show that, for sufficiently smooth signals, hierarchical interpolation can approximate arbitrary long horizons. We also confirm, through thorough experiments on standard long-horizon datasets, that our approach largely outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods. For example, on the M4, our approach on average improves forecast accuracy by almost 20% points in comparison to the state-of-the-art Transformer based approaches, at the cost of less than half the computational resources. Our code is available at https://github.com/Nixtla/neuralforecast.
